Prompting vs RAGs vs Fine-tuning:

An important decision that every AI Engineer must make when building an LLM-based application.

To understand what guides the decision, let's first understand the meaning of these terms.

 Prompting Engineering:

The prompt is the text input

Prompt engineering is slowly dying

Enter 𝗖𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗲𝘅𝘁 𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 

(Maybe not quite dead — but evolving into something far more powerful)

As AI grows from simple chatbots to 𝗮𝘂𝘁𝗼𝗻𝗼𝗺𝗼𝘂𝘀 𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀, clever prompts alone will not be enough. The real

𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗺𝗽𝘁 𝗲𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗶𝘀 𝗱𝗲𝗮𝗱.
I know that sounds dramatic, but hear me out.

Foundations: Core LLM Engineering

Google Research just proved you can boost llm accuracy by up to 76 percentage points with zero extra output tokens, zero latency increase, and zero fine-tuning 

the technique: paste your prompt twice.

that's it. that's the paper.

but WHY it works reveals something important
Key Prompt Engineering Techniques and Why it Makes a Big Difference in LLMs

You can’t “prompt engineer” your way out of bad context.

Real-world LLM systems need both prompt and context engineering.

But what is the actual difference between them?

𝗣𝗿𝗼𝗺𝗽𝘁 𝗘𝗻𝗴𝗶𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴: 
Prompt engineering is about 𝗵𝗼𝘄 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗽𝗵𝗿𝗮𝘀𝗲

Lots of LLM benchmarks are misleading us.

The problem isn't the benchmarks themselves. It has more to do with the prompts. Current evaluation frameworks use fixed prompts that systematically underestimate what models can actually do.

Pay attention to this one, AI devs!

This

LLMs often generate excessively long reasoning steps ("overthinking").

This increases computational costs and response times.

This survey categorizes methods to make LLM reasoning shorter and faster while maintaining accuracy.

Solutions optimize the model itself, modify

Smart prompt engineering beats expensive model fine-tuning for LLM alignment

A prompt optimizer framework that theoretically shows how prompt engineering can match fine-tuning's performance for LLM alignment without parameter updates.
